texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Post-combustion capture (PCC) of carbon dioxide (CO2) is the major option that can be rapidly deployed for reducing CO2 emissions substantially from existing power plants and may also be the choice for power plants to be built in the future (Abu-Zahra et al., 2007). For large scale PCC, absorption and regeneration is the method of choice. But the key question is the process economics, which is primarily dominated by the choice of the solvent (Herzog et al., 2009). Development of new solvent in PCC with economic objective requires pilot plant testing, process heat integration, modelling and simulation, process intensification and government policy (Wang et al., 2011). Several technology developers and vendors have been developing PCC solvents for coal power plants since coal plays an essential role in the electricity generation and will presumably remain one of the most important energy sources in the world's future energy mix. texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, a simulation study of CO2 capture process using aqueous blend of (AMP + PZ) solvents has been presented. CO2 capture from the flue gas stream of a coal fired power plant, using the absorptionregeneration process has been simulated using RadFrac-RateSep block in Aspen Plus ® platform. Aqueous (AMP + PZ) solvents of 30-50 wt% total amine concentration have been considered for this simulation study. Thermodynamic and kinetic parameters of CO2 in aqueous (AMP + PZ) solvent taken from our previous work as well as from literature are used to simulate the flow sheet of CO2 capture process in Aspen Plus. The model results have been validated with the pilot plant results from literature on CO2 capture using aqueous AMP and (AMP + PZ) solvents. The model simulates the temperature profile and the rich and lean loadings of the absorber and regenerator. An optimum set of process conditions, e.g., amine concentration in the aqueous solvent, liquid-to-gas ratio in the absorber, absorber and regenerator height and lean and rich loading have been determined. The Aspen absorber-stripper flow sheet model has been used to study the effects of gas and liquid flow rates, temperature approach in the lean-rich heat exchanger on the extent of CO2 capture, CO2 purity and energy requirement. From this study it is observed, 90% of CO2 removal can be achieved with (18 wt% AMP + 17.5 wt% PZ) solvent for an absorber L / G of 2.9, 10 m of absorber packing height, and a regenerator energy requirement of 3700 kJ/kg CO2.

Aqueous (AMP + PZ) solvents of 30-50 wt% total amine concentration have been considered for this simulation study. Thermodynamic and kinetic parameters of CO2 in aqueous (AMP + PZ) solvent taken from our previous work as well as from literature are used to simulate the flow sheet of CO2 capture process in Aspen Plus. The model results have been validated with the pilot plant results from literature on CO2 capture using aqueous AMP and (AMP + PZ) solvents. The model simulates the temperature profile and the rich and lean loadings of the absorber and regenerator. An optimum set of process conditions, e.g., amine concentration in the aqueous solvent, liquid-to-gas ratio in the absorber, absorber and regenerator height and lean and rich loading have been determined. The Aspen absorber-stripper flow sheet model has been used to study the effects of gas and liquid flow rates, temperature approach in the lean-rich heat exchanger on the extent of CO2 capture, CO2 purity and energy requirement. From this study it is observed, 90% of CO2 removal can be achieved with (18 wt% AMP + 17.5 wt% PZ) solvent for an absorber L / G of 2.9, 10 m of absorber packing height, and a regenerator energy requirement of 3700 kJ/kg CO2.

texts.orig: Siemens POSTCAP amino acid salt based solvents (Jockenhoevel et al., 2010) and hindered amine based KM-CDR or KS-1 (Mitsubishi Heavy Industries/Kansai Electric Corporation) solvents are popular (Kishimoto et al., 2008). A European project coordinated by TNO has developed solvents based on sterically hindered amines and diamine. The solvent is known as CESAR-1 solvent (CO2 Enhanced Separation and Recovery, CESAR, 2011) for PCC. Mangalapally and Hasse (2011) reported that the CESAR-1 solvent is an aqueous blend of AMP and PZ which showed a reduction of about 20% in the regeneration energy and 45% in the solvent circulation-rate compared to those of MEA based CO2 capture process under similar process condition. An important requirement of CO2 chemical absorbent is resistance to thermal and oxidative degradation. Thermal degradation is due to high temperature operation in the stripper and oxidative degradation comes from the presence of oxygen in the flue gas. A number of studies shows that AMP and PZ based solvents are more resistant to thermal and oxidative degradation. The sterically hindered amine AMP has the highest stability against thermal degradation (Eide-Haugmo et al., 2011). Freeman et al. texts.orig: 100 times lower than MEA. PZ also degrades slower at high temperatures than 7 m AMP (Freeman et al., 2010b). With respect to the blended solvent, Freeman and Rochelle (2012) reported that (6 m AMP+4 m PZ) solvent was thermally stable up to 135 ◦ C stripper temperature at a loading of 0.4 mol of CO2/mole of alkalinity (AMP + PZ). Davis (2009) studied thermal and oxidative degradation of amines used in PCC solvent. He reported that AMP is likely to be the most resistant to oxidation in a real system, followed by PZ and PZ derivatives, then methyldiethanolamine (MDEA), and then MEA. Their study reveals that AMP lowers the degradation rate by 75% when compared with MEA at 135 ◦ C. He further reported that very little AMP degradation occurred in experiments conducted at 100 ◦ C and 120 ◦ C. From these reports sterically hindered amine AMP activated with PZ appears to be a potential solvent for CO2 capture.

texts.text: 2. Process flow-sheet and model development

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Gabrielsen (2007) presented a study of CO2 absorption into AMP solution in a pilot plant using structured packing in the absorber and stripper at the Norwegian University of Science and Technology (NTNU). After analyzing the reported pilot plant data, he found that there was only 20% removal of the inlet CO2 using a 2.9 M aqueous AMP. Very recently, Mangalapally and Hasse (2011) presented a pilot plant study of the CO2 capture process using (AMP + PZ) blended solvent with an objective of commercialization of (AMP + PZ) process. They reported that (AMP + PZ) is a promising solvent for CO2 capture from the flue gas stream of coal based as well as natural gas based power plants. They also reported that (AMP + PZ) solvent has an expected reduction of about 20% in the regeneration energy and 45% in the solvent circulation rate over those of the MEA process. To explore further these benefits of the (AMP + PZ) system, a simulation study of CO2 capture process using a commercial simulator has been considered in this work.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Experimental and theoretical investigation of the absorption of CO2 into PZ activated concentrated aqueous AMP is presented elsewhere by these authors (Dash et al., 2011a). The VLE of CO2 into aqueous solutions of (AMP + PZ) has also been presented (Dash et al., 2011b). In this work, an Aspen Plus ® rate-based model of the CO2 capture process using aqueous blend of AMP and PZ has been presented. Thermophysical property models and reaction kinetics models are based on the recent work of Bishnoi and Rochelle (2002) and Dash et al. (2011a). The present study shows that the energy demand strongly depends on the specific process conditions such as CO2 partial pressure in the flue gas and regenerator out let gas (pure CO2), L / G , lean loading in the reboiler and design aspects, e.g., inter-cooling, packing height and properties, etc.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The CO2 capture process used in this study is shown in Fig. 1. The flue gas enters the bottom of the absorber while the lean amine solvent is introduced at the top of the absorber. The lean amine enters with a loading of 0.1-0.12 and leaves with a loading close to 0.45-0.55. In this case, lean amine refers to the regenerated amine stream stripped of CO2. The amine stream loaded with CO2 after absorption is referred to as the rich amine stream. The rich amine leaves from the bottom of the absorber and is pumped through the lean-rich-heat exchanger. In the heat exchanger, the rich stream from the absorber exchanges heat with the lean stream from the regenerator. This allows the rich stream to be heated and the lean stream to be cooled down. Before proceeding to the absorber, the lean stream is further cooled to bring its temperature down to around 45 ◦ C. The rich stream from the lean-rich heat exchanger is fed to the regenerator. The regenerator is a packed column with a kettle reboiler. The regenerator typically operates at slightly elevated pressures ( ∼ 180-202 kPa). The rich amine enters the second stage of the regenerator and flows down the column. The vapour from the reboiler rises counter-currently to the liquid. The gas and vapour stream from the top of the column is taken to a condenser in order to condense the water and amine vapour and lower the temperature. The condensate is then flashed in order to separate CO2 from water and amine. A part of the liquid reflux is returned to the first stage of the regenerator column. The first stage in the regenerator acts as a water wash stage to return any entrained amine in the vapour to the reboiler. In the reboiler of the regenerator, steam from the power plant is used to furnish the required heat duty.

texts.orig: In order to simulate the process in Aspen Plus the absorber and regenerator are modeled by RadFrac, using RateSep model packed column block, in which a packing that provides sufficient surface area for the absorption of CO2 has been used (Aspen Plus, 2011). The Mellapak ® structured packing Y125 has been considered for both absorption and regeneration columns. The lean-rich heat exchanger is modeled using Aspen ® Heatx model. The exchanger approach temperature is set to 5 ◦ C for optimal use of the heat exchanger. Apart from this, it is necessary to maintain water balance in the plant in order to have closed loop convergence. For this purpose, the flow rate of the make-up water stream is varied to achieve an overall water balance. The specification of the unit operation block (RadFrac-RateSep) is presented in Table 1. A number of design specifications for the reboiler duty and the lean-rich heat exchanger are incorporated to converge the flow sheet in a closed loop. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1. Rate based modelling in Aspen Plus
texts.text: 2.1. Rate based modelling in Aspen Plus

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work the absorber was modeled with RadFrac, using RateSep model, a rate-based model frame work in Aspen Plus ® . Aspen RateSep allows for the rate-based calculations of absorption and regeneration columns. It is a stage based model and allows the calculations of mass and heat transfer phenomena as well as the kinetics of chemical reactions. In the present work, reaction kinetics are combined with a rigorous thermodynamic model based on the electrolyte NRTL theory (ENRTL). The various equations solved in Aspen RateSep include mass and heat balances for the vapour and liquid phases, mass and heat transfer rate models to determine interphase transfer rates, vapour-liquid equilibrium equations for the interphase equilibria, and estimation of mass and heat transfer coefficients and interfacial areas as proposed by Bravo et al. (1985). The liquid holdup correlations and other relevant coefficients are obtained using the default correlations of RateSep (Aspen Plus, 2011).

Aspen RateSep allows for the rate-based calculations of absorption and regeneration columns. It is a stage based model and allows the calculations of mass and heat transfer phenomena as well as the kinetics of chemical reactions. In the present work, reaction kinetics are combined with a rigorous thermodynamic model based on the electrolyte NRTL theory (ENRTL). The various equations solved in Aspen RateSep include mass and heat balances for the vapour and liquid phases, mass and heat transfer rate models to determine interphase transfer rates, vapour-liquid equilibrium equations for the interphase equilibria, and estimation of mass and heat transfer coefficients and interfacial areas as proposed by Bravo et al. (1985). The liquid holdup correlations and other relevant coefficients are obtained using the default correlations of RateSep (Aspen Plus, 2011).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: While formulating an absorber-regenerator flow sheet on Aspen Plus, we require to provide the necessary physical property parameters and equilibrium and kinetic parameters with the chosen property method relevant to the system (CO2 + AMP + PZ + H2O). The property parameters are often regressed from experimental data. The VLE chosen for this case is based on ENRTL theory for the liquid phase and Redlich-Kwong-Soave equation of state for the gas phase. The physical property parameters and ENRTL interaction parameters for the VLE model and Henry's constants for CO2 in water and AMP have been used from the Aspen data bank as well as regressed results from literature (Dash et al., 2012). In the reaction calculations, the activity coefficient basis for the Henry's components is chosen to be aqueous phase. Therefore, in calculating the unsymmetric activity coefficients of the solutes, the infinite dilution activity coefficients have been calculated based on infinite-dilution condition in pure water. The parameters of liquid molar volume model (Chen et al., 2001) and transport property models have been regressed and validated against available experimental data in the literature (Aspen Plus, 2011). While formulating the rate-based simulation, kinetics of the reactions are required. The kinetic parameters obtained by regressing experimental data of this work and presented elsewhere (Dash et al., 2011a) have been used to develop the rate based model for CO2 absorption in (AMP + PZ + H2O). The power law model (Eq. (1)) is used to represent the rate of reaction for the rate-controlled reactions. The kinetic parameters used in this model are presented in Table 2.

texts.orig: where r is the rate constant, k 0 is the pre-exponential factor, E is activation energy, R is gas law constant, C is concentration in molarity. Aspen RadFrac-RateSep block allows the calculation of reaction rate in the film. For systems in which there are rapid reactions, it is necessary to discretize the film properly in order to quantify for the amount of reaction in the film accurately (Asprion, 2006). If the reaction is very fast and the film is not discretized, then RadFrac-RateSep will calculate the extent of reaction in the film based on the concentration at the interface (Kothandaraman, 2010). This will be higher than the actual film reaction and hence will not accurately model the system. Also, the number of discretization points should be such that the mathematical solution is stable while optimizing on the computation time. This process of optimizing the film discretization has been performed for (AMP + PZ) systems. For other calculations, Aspen default procedure has been followed.

texts.text: 2.2. Simulation approach

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The flue gas from a power plant mainly consists of nitrogen, water vapour and CO2. Therefore, in this study, simulated flue gas comprising of CO2, N2 and H2O has been used. In addition to the main components, the flue gas also contains some undesirable components, e.g., oxygen as well as NO x and SO x . It is known that these components may react with solvents to produce heat stable salts requiring substantial solvent make up. The present model can also include these components for performance study of the solvent. Typical values of the CO2 partial pressure are 4-6 kPa for gas-fired power plants and 10-14 kPa for coal-fired power plants. This study has been performed considering a CO2 partial pressure of 13 kPa.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the rate-based calculations, it is necessary to provide the diameter and height of the column. The diameter of the column has been determined based on flooding approach. A design factor of 80% approach to flooding has been chosen, since this also allows for some safety factors in case the operating conditions change suddenly. There is also a limit set on the pressure drop of the column and this translates into a constraint on the calculation of the diameter of the column. As the CO2 capture rate depends on process parameters like L / G ratio, lean loading, etc., the desired extent of capture has been achieved by varying the amine flow rate and lean process.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3. Model validation with pilot plant data
texts.text: 2.3. Model validation with pilot plant data

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Gabrielsen (2007) conducted study of CO2 absorption into a 2.9 mol dm -3 AMP solution in a pilot plant using structured packing in both absorber and stripper. He reported experimental data for eleven runs, including temperature profiles for both the absorber and the stripper and CO2 concentrations in both gas and liquid phases at the inlet and outlet of the absorber and stripper. The absorber was operated at atmospheric pressure and the stripper at approximately 202 kPa. Mangalapally and Hasse (2011) reported pilot plant study of CO2 capture in aqueous (28 wt% AMP + 17 wt% PZ).
texts.text: Gabrielsen (2007) conducted study of CO2 absorption into a 2.9 mol dm -3 AMP solution in a pilot plant using structured packing in both absorber and stripper. He reported experimental data for eleven runs, including temperature profiles for both the absorber and the stripper and CO2 concentrations in both gas and liquid phases at the inlet and outlet of the absorber and stripper. The absorber was operated at atmospheric pressure and the stripper at approximately 202 kPa. Mangalapally and Hasse (2011) reported pilot plant study of CO2 capture in aqueous (28 wt% AMP + 17 wt% PZ).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Incorporating the VLE and kinetic parameters obtained in this work, the model is used to simulate the flow sheet in Aspen Plus. The simulation results are compared with the reported pilot plant data of Gabrielsen (2007) and Mangalapally and Hasse (2011). Preliminary simulations identified the need for correction factors in order to achieve closer agreement between the model prediction and pilot plant results. To correct the heat of absorption, and hence temperature profile, a correction factor is applied to the activation energy of CO2 and PZ reaction to generate a more reasonable temperature profile. To correct the VLE predictions at high temperature, a correction factor is applied to Henrys' constant for the mixed solvent. Following the adjustments of these parameters, the CO2 capture process simulation is successfully validated against an independent set of steady state pilot plant data. Typical simulation results with respect to pilot plant data of Gabrielsen (2007) is presented in Table 3. The average absolute deviation (AAD) between pilot plant data for 30 wt% AMP (Gabrielsen, 2007) and the simulation results are between 2 and 7%. The AAD between pilot plant data for aqueous (28 wt% AMP + 18 wt% PZ) solvent (Mangalapally and Hasse, 2011) and the simulation results are between 4 and 12%.

texts.orig: 2.4. Parametric study
texts.text: 2.4. Parametric study

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The validated flow sheet of the CO2 capture process is used to study the effect of various parameters considering the solvent compositions of 30, 40 and 50 wt% total (AMP + PZ). For all these studies, the flue gas flow rate of about 1000 kmol/h is considered with 13 mol% CO2, 4 mol% water vapour and rest nitrogen. An optimum set of process conditions, e.g., amine concentration in aqueous solvent of (AMP + PZ), liquid-to-gas ratio ( L / G ) in the absorber, lean and rich loadings, inlet/outlet CO2 partial pressure, temperature approach in the lean-rich heat exchanger have been found out. The simulation results and the results of parametric study of absorber/regenerator performance using various blends of aqueous (AMP + PZ) solvent of this study are presented here.

texts.orig: 2.7. Effect of column pressure on temperature profile
texts.text: 2.7. Effect of column pressure on temperature profile

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 6 shows the effect of column pressure on temperature profile in the absorber column in the pressure range of (101-2020) kPa. The column temperature increases with column pressure. This is possible due to the increased heat of reaction with increase in the column pressure. The temperature maxima are typical of absorption columns in PCC processes and caused by the release of absorption enthalpy. Kvamsdal and Rochelle (2008) reported that the location of the temperature profile peak and in general the shape of the temperature profile depends on L / G ratio and amount of CO2 absorbed. It is observed that the temperature does not increase appreciably when the total pressure of the column is 101 kPa. This is further supported by pilot plant data reported for AMP (Gabrielsen, 2007) and (AMP + PZ) solvents (Mangalapally and Hasse, 2011). The temperature difference in the case of 30 wt% AMP is in the range of 5-10 ◦ C depending on gas and liquid flow rates. The temperature difference in the case of (28 wt% AMP + 17 wt% PZ) is in the range of 15-20 ◦ C at an L / G (kg/kg) of 1.25-2.9. In case of high column pressure most of the CO2 absorption takes place in the lower part of the absorber (due to high CO2 partial pressure at inlet as reported by Kvamsdal and Rochelle, 2008) resulting in a larger temperature bulge. As a result, high liquid temperatures are observed in the absorber bottom for the high pressure case. However, at low column pressure, the temperature bulge is smaller and it is expected that the temperature maxima is nearer to the top of the column, since most of the CO2 absorption takes place in the upper part of the absorber. This observation is also supported by the experimental results of Mangalapally and Hasse (2011).

texts.orig: A set of simulation run has been carried out to find out the effect of packing height on CO2 capture at a constant L / G for various (AMP + PZ) mixed solvents. The results are presented in Figs. 8 and 9 for (22 wt% AMP + 8 wt% PZ) and (32 wt% AMP + 8 wt% PZ), respectively. It is clear from Figs. 8 and 9 that with increasing column height, the %CO2 capture increase up to a packing height of 20 m and then remains unchanged. This may be due to the attainment of equilibrium partial pressure at the top of the absorber column corresponding to the lean loading of CO2.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.12. Regenerator study
texts.text: 2.12. Regenerator study

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Regenerator optimization can be performed by varying the degree of solvent regeneration and finding out the minimum thermal energy requirement for solvent regeneration. Notz et al. (2011) reported that the minimum heat requirement for the regeneration of the solvent in the regenerator is obtained when equilibrium is reached in the regenerator. Equilibrium may be reached in the regenerator of a CO2 capture process, provided that a fast reacting solvent is used and regeneration is done under elevated pressure and temperatures. Further, the choice of the number of equilibrium stages for the representation of the regenerator model is based on the minimum number of stages necessary to ensure that equilibrium is reached as suggested by Oyenekan and Rochelle (2006). The model predicted regenerator operating conditions and temperature profiles as a function of column height is shown in Fig. 12. From the simulation study, it is found that with six equilibrium stages each of 1 m height, CO2 in the gas phase would reach equilibrium with that in the liquid phase. It is clear from Fig. 12 that the equilibrium and operating lines are converging towards the bottom of the column.

texts.orig: The reboiler heat duty represents the largest fraction of the overall energy requirement in a PCC process (Kim and Svendsen, 2011). The reboiler heat duty is therefore an important quantity which needs to be considered carefully. The energy requirement for solvent regeneration can be split into three components. Energy to be supplied (i) to provide the desorption enthalpy of CO2, (ii) to generate additional stripping steam, and (iii) for heating up the solvent and provide the condensate reflux (sensible heat). Fig. 15 presents the effect of %CO2 capture on reboiler duty (kJ/kg CO2) at constant lean loading with a temperature approach at the lean-rich heat exchanger of 5 ◦ C. It is observed from Figs. 14 and 15 that with the increase in %CO2 capture, the reboiler duty increases as well. There is a steep increase of reboilor duty with the %CO2 capture approaching 95%. Clearly this is due to the higher sensible heat requirement for the solvent for the higher circulation rate. The energy requirement with respect to solvent circulation rate and lean loading is found to be 3.9 GJ/ton-CO2 at L / G of 2.75 and a lean loading of 0.129 for 90% CO2 capture. However, the energy requirement is 3.75 GJ/ton-CO2 at L / G of 2.9 and a lean loading of 0.155 for the same conditions. Hence, the energy demand is less when the lean loading is adjusted to achieve the desired % capture rather than by varying the L / G ratio.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 16 presents the total energy requirement as the sum of sensible heat, heat of vaporization and heat of absorption. The major share of total energy requirement is the energy needed to supply the desorption enthalpy of CO2 (heat of absorption). For 90% capture this value is about 1.58 GJ/ton-CO2 for this solvent. It is found that the heat of absorption for an aqueous solution of (AMP + PZ) is almost independent of the CO2 loading up to a value of about 0.5 mol CO2/mol amine. The small difference results from the temperature effect on the heat of absorption. The energy required for generating stripping steam is 1.49 GJ/ton-CO2. The energy demand for stripping steam increases with the increase in %CO2 capture, i.e., with the decrease in lean loading, since a reduction of the equilibrium CO2 partial pressure of the lean solution requires an increased amount of stripping steam to realize a more thorough regeneration. The sensible heat contribution is only 0.66 GJ/tonCO2. Sensible heat requirement decreases to some extent with the increase in stripping steam. The total energy requirement is about 3.7 GJ/ton-CO2 from this simulation study. This value is little higher compared to the pilot plant results reported by Mangalapally and Hasse (2011).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3. Conclusion
texts.text: 3. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this report, the effectiveness of the aqueous (AMP + PZ) as a new solvent for the removal of CO2 by chemical absorption has been studied through a simulation work. A parametric study of the CO2 capture process from flue gas of a coal fired power plant is presented. It is based on the absorption-regeneration process with (AMP + PZ) solutions. For this the rate based model of Aspen Plus ® has been used. This simulation study indicates that 90% CO2 capture can be achieved by using (22 wt% AMP + 8 wt% PZ) with an L / G of 4.25, using (32 wt% AMP + 8 wt% PZ) with an L / G of 3.75 and by using (18 wt% AMP + 17.5 wt% PZ) with an L / G of 2.9, while the lean loading is 0.1 and the packing height is 10 m.
texts.text: In this report, the effectiveness of the aqueous (AMP + PZ) as a new solvent for the removal of CO2 by chemical absorption has been studied through a simulation work. A parametric study of the CO2 capture process from flue gas of a coal fired power plant is presented. It is based on the absorption-regeneration process with (AMP + PZ) solutions. For this the rate based model of Aspen Plus ® has been used. This simulation study indicates that 90% CO2 capture can be achieved by using (22 wt% AMP + 8 wt% PZ) with an L / G of 4.25, using (32 wt% AMP + 8 wt% PZ) with an L / G of 3.75 and by using (18 wt% AMP + 17.5 wt% PZ) with an L / G of 2.9, while the lean loading is 0.1 and the packing height is 10 m.

texts.orig: The optimum solvent composition should be based on circulation rate and the energy demand considering the solubility limit of PZ in the aqueous solvent.
texts.text: The optimum solvent composition should be based on circulation rate and the energy demand considering the solubility limit of PZ in the aqueous solvent.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The CO2 capture model developed in Aspen Plus is used for evaluating the effects of varying amine circulation rate, packing height in absorber and stripper, temperature and pressure of columns, reboilor duty, etc. The developed model can be improved further to improve the chemical absorption process for CO2 capture with the optimization of absorber, stripper, lean-rich heat exchanger and different flow sheet configuration. Since implementation of CO2 absorption/stripping system in a coal fired power plant will introduce significant capital and operating costs, other options to lower the total cost of capture should also be studied. One model for the power plant and another for CO2 capture and appropriately combining the two may be effective for reducing the total energy demand and for overall cost optimization.
